1: Majura Parkway is an 11.5-kilometre-long north-south parkway under construction in the Majura district of the Australian Capital Territory.
The project has been jointly funded by both the ACT and Australian Governments, at a total cost of A$288 million.
2: Highway 400 is a 400-series highway in Ontario linking the city of Toronto with the scenic and sparsely populated central and northern regions.
It was the first fully controlled-access highway in Ontario when it was opened between North York and Barrie on July 1, 1952.
3: The Greenville Bridge is a cable-stayed bridge over the Mississippi River carrying US 82 and US 278.
Construction was begun in 2001 and the new bridge opened in 2010.
4: The Arroyo Seco Parkway is the first freeway in the Western United States.
It connects Los Angeles with Pasadena alongside the Arroyo Seco seasonal river.
5: The Bay Freeway was a proposed elevated freeway in the South Lake Union neighborhood of Seattle.
Planning for the freeway began in 1954, with the proposal for a freeway from Elliott Bay to the Central Freeway.
6: The M54 is a 23-mile east-west motorway in the English counties of Shropshire and Staffordshire.
The motorway forms part of the strategic route to North Wales.
7: The A9 motorway is a north-south motorway in Croatia, with a length of 78.3 kilometres.
The motorway represents a significant north-south transportation corridor in Croatia.
8: North West Coastal Highway links the coastal city of Geraldton with the town of Port Hedland.
It supports the agricultural, pastoral, fishing, and tourism industries.
9: The A4232, also known as the Peripheral Distributor Road, is a distributor road in Cardiff.
Construction of the first phase of the final link road started on 17 March 2016.
10: The Lincoln Alexander Parkway is a municipal expressway in Hamilton, Ontario.
The expressway opened on October 15, 1997, just over a decade before the controversial Red Hill Valley Parkway.

topic.name: Major League Baseball Players
topic.description: The documents discuss the careers and lives of various Major League Baseball players, highlighting their achievements and personal stories.

Document 1: Joe Borden was a starting pitcher in professional baseball for two seasons.
He threw the first no-hitter in professional baseball history.
Document 2: Byron McLaughlin is an American retired professional baseball player.
His baseball career spanned nine seasons in Major League Baseball.
Document 3: Daniel Thomas Meyer is a retired professional baseball player whose career spanned 15 seasons.
He played 12 of those seasons in Major League Baseball.
Document 4: David Martinez is an American professional baseball coach and former outfielder.
He played in MLB for multiple teams from 1986 to 2001.
Document 5: Daniel Charles Shay was a professional baseball player who played in Major League Baseball.
He played all or part of four seasons in MLB.
Document 6: Lewis Robert Wilson was an American Major League Baseball player who played 12 seasons.
He was one of the most accomplished power hitters in the game during the late 1920s.
Document 7: Alva Lee 'Bobo' Holloman was an American right-handed pitcher in Major League Baseball.
He threw a no-hitter in his first start.
Document 8: Joseph Bert Tinker was an American professional baseball player and manager.
He played from 1902 through 1916 for the Chicago Cubs and Cincinnati Reds.
Document 9: Curtis 'Curt' Benjamin Roberts was an American baseball second baseman who played three seasons for the Pittsburgh Pirates.
He was the first black Major League player for the Pirates.
Document 10: Tyler Lee Clippard is an American professional baseball pitcher for the Arizona Diamondbacks.
He previously played for several Major League teams.

The state highway was established in 1924.
Document 3: State Route 365 ( SR 365 ) is a very short two-lane east – west state highway in western Ohio.
This spur route was created in 1934.
Document 7: Maryland Route 743 ( MD 743 ) is a state highway in the U.S. state of Maryland.
The state highway runs 1.00 mile ( 1.61 km ) from MD 36 in Frostburg east to U.S. Route 40 Alternate.
Document 9: Bridge No. 1860 , also known as the Samson Occom Bridge , is a fieldstone arch bridge in Montville, Connecticut.

topic.name: Australian Military History
topic.description: This cluster focuses on significant events, units, and figures in the history of the Australian military, particularly during World War II.

Document 1: North-Western Area Command was one of several geographically based commands raised by the Royal Australian Air Force ( RAAF ) during World War II.
Its wartime sphere of operations included the Northern Territory, adjacent portions of Queensland and Western Australia.
Document 2: The 2 / 10th Battalion was an infantry battalion of the Australian Army that was raised for overseas service as part of the all volunteer Second Australian Imperial Force ( 2nd AIF ) during World War II.
Formed in October 1939, it was the first 2nd AIF unit raised from the state of South Australia during the war.
Document 3: Air Chief Marshal Sir Neville Patrick McNamara was a senior commander of the Royal Australian Air Force ( RAAF ).
He joined the RAAF during World War II and saw action in the South West Pacific.
Document 5: Wilfred Stanley Arthur was a fighter ace and senior officer of the Royal Australian Air Force ( RAAF ) during World War II.
He first saw action with No. 3 Squadron in North Africa.
Document 6: No. 5 Airfield Construction Squadron was a Royal Australian Air Force ( RAAF ) construction squadron that served in the New Guinea Campaign and Borneo Campaign during World War II.
The squadron was one of only a few RAAF airfield construction units to be retained at the end of the war.

topic.name: Medieval Church Figures
topic.description: The documents discuss notable individuals in the medieval church, their roles, conflicts, and contributions to ecclesiastical and political life in England and beyond.

1: Reginald Fitz Jocelin was a medieval Bishop of Bath and an Archbishop of Canterbury-elect in England.
He attended the Third Lateran Council in 1179, and spent much of his time administering his diocese.
3: Burchard du Puiset was a medieval Anglo-Norman clergyman and treasurer of the diocese of York.
His appointment was opposed by the newly appointed Archbishop Geoffrey, which led to a long dispute.
6: Ceolnoth was a medieval English Archbishop of Canterbury.
He faced two problems as archbishop — raids and invasions by the Vikings and a new political situation.

topic.name: Video Game Development and Culture
topic.description: The documents explore various aspects of video game development, notable games, and the cultural impact of the gaming industry.

Document 1: Minecraft is a sandbox video game originally created by Swedish programmer Markus 'Notch' Persson and later developed and published by Mojang.
Minecraft received five awards during the 2011 Game Developers Conference.
Document 2: Grand Theft Auto III is an open world action-adventure video game developed by DMA Design and published by Rockstar Games.
The game was delayed following the September 11 attacks, to allow the team to change references and gameplay deemed inappropriate.
Document 3: The Atari video game burial was a mass burial of unsold video game cartridges, consoles, and computers in a New Mexico landfill site.
The event had become a cultural icon and a reminder of the North American video game crash of 1983.
Document 4: The seventh generation includes consoles released since late 2005 by Nintendo, Microsoft, and Sony Computer Entertainment.
Video game consoles had become an important part of the global IT infrastructure.
Document 5: The Operative: No One Lives Forever is a first-person shooter video game with stealth gameplay elements, developed by Monolith Productions and published by Fox Interactive.
The game was critically acclaimed for its stylistic representation of the era in the spirit of many spy films.
Document 6: The development of L.A. Noire began in 2004 following the founding of its developer Team Bondi.
L.A. Noire was notable for being the first to use the newly developed MotionScan technology.
Document 7: Fallout 3 is an action role-playing open world video game developed by Bethesda Game Studios.
Fallout 3 was critically acclaimed and received a number of Game of the Year awards.
Document 8: Bungie, Inc. is an American video game developer located in Bellevue, Washington, United States.
Halo became the Xbox's 'killer application', selling millions of copies and spawning a billion dollar franchise.
Document 9: The development of Red Dead Redemption began in 2005.
The game uses the Euphoria and Bullet engines for further animation and environment rendering tasks.
Document 10: Insomniac Games, Inc. is an American video game developer whose corporate headquarters is located in Burbank, California.
Insomniac Games has received recognition from critics as an acclaimed video game developer.

topic.name: American Revolutionary War Battles
topic.description: The documents discuss various significant battles and military engagements that occurred during the American Revolutionary War, highlighting key events, strategies, and outcomes.

Document 1: The Battle of Oriskany, fought on August 6, 1777, was one of the bloodiest battles in the North American theater of the American Revolutionary War.
An American party trying to relieve the siege of Fort Stanwix was ambushed by a party of Loyalists and allies of several Native American tribes.
Document 2: The Battle of Short Hills was a conflict between a Continental Army force commanded by Brigadier General William Alexander and an opposing British force commanded by Lieutenant General William Howe.
The battle took place on June 26, 1777, at Scotch Plains and Metuchen, New Jersey, during the American Revolutionary War.
Document 3: The Battle of Antietam, fought on September 17, 1862, near Sharpsburg, Maryland, was the first major battle in the American Civil War to take place on Union soil.
It is the bloodiest single-day battle in American history, with a combined tally of 22,717 dead, wounded, or missing.
Document 4: The Siege of Fort William Henry was conducted in August 1757 by French General Louis-Joseph de Montcalm against the British-held Fort William Henry.
The fort was garrisoned by a poorly supported force of British regulars and provincial militia led by Lieutenant Colonel George Monro.
Document 5: George Rogers Clark was a surveyor, soldier, and militia officer from Virginia and the highest ranking American military officer on the northwestern frontier during the American Revolutionary War.
He is best known for his celebrated captures of Kaskaskia (1778) and Vincennes (1779) during the Illinois Campaign.
Document 6: The Siege of Boston was the opening phase of the American Revolutionary War.
New England militiamen prevented the movement by land of the British Army garrisoned in what was then the peninsular city of Boston, Massachusetts.
Document 7: The Battle of Bound Brook was a surprise attack conducted by British and Hessian forces against a Continental Army outpost at Bound Brook, New Jersey during the American Revolutionary War.
The British objective of capturing the entire garrison was not met, although prisoners were taken.
Document 9: The Battle of Trenton was a small but pivotal battle during the American Revolutionary War which took place on the morning of December 26, 1776, in Trenton, New Jersey.
Washington led the main body of the Continental Army against Hessian soldiers garrisoned at Trenton.

topic.name: Electricity and Energy Systems
topic.description: The documents discuss various aspects of electricity generation, transmission, and energy efficiency technologies.

Document 1: Hydro-Québec 's electricity transmission system is an international power transmission system centred in Quebec , Canada.
The system pioneered the use of very high voltage 735 kV alternating current ( AC ) power lines.
Document 2: A variable-frequency drive ( VFD ) is a type of adjustable-speed drive used in electro-mechanical drive systems to control AC motor speed and torque.
VFDs are used in applications ranging from small appliances to the largest of mine mill drives and compressors.

topic.name: Australian Cricket History
topic.description: This topic encompasses the history and notable figures of Australian cricket, including players, series, and significant events.

1: Jack Fingleton was an Australian cricketer who represented Australia in 18 Tests between 1932 and 1938.
Fingleton became a prominent political commentator in Canberra, with links to Australian prime ministers.
2: Norm O'Neill was a cricketer who played for New South Wales and Australia, scoring three Test centuries.
O'Neill was named as one of the Wisden Cricketers of the Year during his career.
3: Bill Johnston was a spearhead of Don Bradman's undefeated 1948 touring team, known as 'The Invincibles'.
Johnston was named by Wisden as one of its Cricketers of the Year in 1949.
4: Jake Seamer was an amateur cricketer who played for Oxford University and Somerset.
Seamer became a district commissioner in the Sudan after his cricket career.
5: The 1948 Ashes series was a cricket rivalry between England and Australia, resulting in a 4-0 series win for Australia.
Australia remained undefeated for their entire tour of England, earning them the sobriquet of The Invincibles.
6: Arnold Fothergill was an English professional cricketer who played first-class cricket for Somerset and the MCC.
Fothergill took 119 first-class wickets during his career, with eight coming in his two Tests.
7: Doug Ring played for Victoria and Australia in 13 Tests from 1948 to 1953.
Ring was part of the famous 'Invincibles' side during the 1948 tour of England.
9: Bill Brown was a member of Donald Bradman's famous Australian cricket team, which toured England in 1948.
Brown's selection as a reserve opener generated controversy among critics.
10: Craig Kieswetter is an English former cricketer who appeared in 71 matches for England between 2010 and 2013.
Kieswetter retired from professional cricket in June 2015 due to an eye injury.

topic.name: Military Contributions of New Zealand and Australia
topic.description: This topic covers the military involvement and historical significance of New Zealand and Australian forces during the World Wars, highlighting specific battalions and campaigns.

Document 1: The 28th ( Māori ) Battalion was an infantry battalion of the New Zealand Army that served during the Second World War.
The Māori Battalion followed in the footsteps of the Māori Pioneer Battalion that served during the First World War with success.
Document 2: The outbreak of World War I was greeted with considerable enthusiasm in Australia.
The first campaign that Australians were involved in was in German New Guinea.
Document 3: The Wellington Mounted Rifles Regiment was a mounted infantry regiment from New Zealand, raised for service during the First World War.
The regiment fought predominantly against the forces of the Ottoman Turkish Empire.
Document 4: The Canterbury Mounted Rifles Regiment was a mounted infantry regiment from New Zealand, raised for service during the First World War.
They participated in the largest battle of that theatre at Chunuk Bair.
Document 5: The 2nd Battalion was an infantry battalion of the Australian Army, initially raised for service during the First World War.
It saw action at Gallipoli before being sent to the Western Front.
Document 6: The 15th Battalion was an infantry battalion of the Australian Army, formed in 1914 as part of the all-volunteer Australian Imperial Force.
The battalion fought during the Gallipoli Campaign and on the Western Front during the First World War.
